facts: This Full Bench reference arose from a Division Bench order in General Manager, KSRTC v. Khatujabee challenging a Tribunal award in a fatal motor-accident case. The Tribunal in Khatujabee had awarded Rs. 2,70,000 as loss of dependency (daily wages Rs.30 less Rs.5 personal expenses = Rs.25; annual Rs.9,000; multiplier 30). The core procedural question referred was whether this Court's earlier Division Bench decision in H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma (which evolved an 'operative multiplier' principle) had been impliedly overruled by two Supreme Court decisions (Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari). The Full Bench examined the scope of Article 141 and multiple authorities to decide whether the Supreme Court decisions constituted 'law declared' on selection of multiplier.
arguments_claimant: The view underpinning the referral argued that the Supreme Court decisions in Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari had laid down principles (including taking normal life-span as 70 years in Jyotsna Dey) that effectively overruled Bhandary's multiplier guidance and required Tribunals to follow the Supreme Court's approach. It was contended that the Division Bench in Khatujabee was correct in treating the Supreme Court pronouncements as binding precedent under Article 141 and therefore Bhandary should no longer be applied.
arguments_respondent: Opposing arguments, accepted by the Full Bench, were that the Supreme Court's conclusions in Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari were rendered in the peculiar facts of those cases and did not constitute a full exposition of law or 'law declared' under Article 141 on the choice of multiplier. It was argued that Bhandary had evolved an 'operative multiplier' principle (basic multiplier 20 reduced to operative 16 for average cases) which remained a binding and useful guide for Tribunals in the State.
ratio_decidendi: "The afore-stated 'operative multiplier' evolved by the Division Bench of this Court in making the choice of multiplier for capitalising the annual loss of dependency to determine the compensation payable to the dependants of the deceased in a motor accident, being the ratio and principle laid down in the decision on a full exposition of the law on the question falling for its consideration, it becomes the law declared by the High Court and a precedent for future cases to be decided by the Tribunals." The Full Bench held that "the afore-mentioned conclusions of the Supreme Court found in its decision in Jyotsna Dey's case (supra) cannot be regarded as 'law declared' under Article 141 of the Constitution." Consequently, the Court decided that "the decision of this Court in H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma ... does not stand overruled by the decisions of the Supreme Court in Rajendra Kumari v. Shanta Trivedi and in Jyotsna Dey and Ors. v. State of Assam and Ors." The Court qualified this by emphasising that Supreme Court observations are binding only insofar as they constitute the ratio and a full exposition of law, and that factual conclusions not framed as general principles cannot be treated as 'law declared'.
final_order: The Full Bench answered the reference by holding that H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma does not stand overruled by the Supreme Court decisions in Rajendra Kumari and Jyotsna Dey, and the contrary view taken by the Division Bench in General Manager, KSRTC v. Khatujabee is overruled.
summary: This is a Karnataka High Court Full Bench reference questioning whether the Court's earlier Bhandary decision on the 'operative multiplier' for loss of dependency in fatal motor-accident cases was impliedly overruled by two Supreme Court decisions (Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari). The Full Bench held that Bhandary's operative multiplier principle (basic multiplier 20, operative 16 for average cases) remains the law declared by the High Court and that the Supreme Court decisions cited were fact-specific and did not constitute 'law declared' under Article 141. The Full Bench overruled the contrary Division Bench decision in Khatujabee and confirmed that Tribunals may continue to apply the operative multiplier guidance.
embedding_summary: O.V. Shanthakumari v. Kokila, High Court of Karnataka Full Bench, 27 Nov 1990 (1991 ACJ 656; ILR 1990 KAR 4324). Reference under Section 7 Karnataka High Court Act, 1961 from General Manager, KSRTC v. Khatujabee concerning a MACT fatal accident award (Tribunal had awarded Rs.2,70,000 using multiplier 30). Core issue: whether H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma (operative multiplier principle: basic multiplier 20, operative multiplier 16 for average cases) was impliedly overruled by Supreme Court decisions Jyotsna Dey [1987(1) ACJ 172] (span of life taken as 70) and Rajendra Kumari (1989 decision). Holding: Bhandary remains law declared by this Court; Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari were fact-specific and not 'law declared' under Article 141; Khatujabee's contrary view is overruled. Key legal points: Article 141 scope, Section 110E MV Act reference to just compensation, multiplier selection adjusted cumulatively for contingencies; no fresh compensation awarded by this judgment.
legal_principles: Operative multiplier principle as law declared by the Karnataka High Court (basic multiplier 20; operative multiplier 16 as highest applicable in average cases)
Supreme Court factual conclusions do not automatically become 'law declared' under Article 141 unless they constitute a full exposition of law
Choice of multiplier must account cumulatively for contingencies and be adjusted (scaling-down) rather than applying a fixed life-span formula rigidly
A decision's ratio, not isolated observations or factual findings, is binding precedent and scope of precedent should not be expanded beyond case facts
Contrary Division Bench decisions applying Supreme Court observations as general rules can be overruled where those observations are not 'law declared'
sections_cited: Section 7 of the Karnataka High Court Act, 1961
Section 110E of the Motor Vehicles Act
cases_cited: H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma, ILR 1985(2) Karnataka 2337
Jyotsna Dey and Ors. v. State of Assam and Ors., [1987(1) ACJ 172]
Rajendra Kumari v. Shanta Trivedi and Ors. (decided 20-2-1989)
General Manager, Karnataka State Road Transport Corporation v. Khatujabee, ILR 1990 KAR 3240
Shamu Rao v. Union Territory, Pondicherry
Madhav Rao Scindia v. Union of India
Amar Nath Om Parkash v. State of Punjab
P.A. Shah v. State of Gujarat
Madhya Pradesh State Road Transport Corporation v. Sudhakar and Ors. (1977 A.C.J. 290)
Davies v. Powell Duffryn Associated Collieries Ltd, (1942) A.C. 601
Nance v. British Colonial Electric Railway Co. Ltd, (1951) A.C. 601
bm25_text: O.V. Shanthakumari vs Kokila High Court of Karnataka (Full Bench) 1990 This Full Bench reference arose from a Division Bench order in General Manager, KSRTC v. Khatujabee challenging a Tribunal award in a fatal motor-accident case. The Tribunal in Khatujabee had awarded Rs. 2,70,000 as loss of dependency (daily wages Rs.30 less Rs.5 personal expenses = Rs.25; annual Rs.9,000; multiplier 30). The core procedural question referred was whether this Court's earlier Division Bench decision in H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma (which evolved an 'operative multiplier' principle) had been impliedly overruled by two Supreme Court decisions (Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari). The Full Bench examined the scope of Article 141 and multiple authorities to decide whether the Supreme Court decisions constituted 'law declared' on selection of multiplier. The view underpinning the referral argued that the Supreme Court decisions in Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari had laid down principles (including taking normal life-span as 70 years in Jyotsna Dey) that effectively overruled Bhandary's multiplier guidance and required Tribunals to follow the Supreme Court's approach. It was contended that the Division Bench in Khatujabee was correct in treating the Supreme Court pronouncements as binding precedent under Article 141 and therefore Bhandary should no longer be applied. Opposing arguments, accepted by the Full Bench, were that the Supreme Court's conclusions in Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari were rendered in the peculiar facts of those cases and did not constitute a full exposition of law or 'law declared' under Article 141 on the choice of multiplier. It was argued that Bhandary had evolved an 'operative multiplier' principle (basic multiplier 20 reduced to operative 16 for average cases) which remained a binding and useful guide for Tribunals in the State. "The afore-stated 'operative multiplier' evolved by the Division Bench of this Court in making the choice of multiplier for capitalising the annual loss of dependency to determine the compensation payable to the dependants of the deceased in a motor accident, being the ratio and principle laid down in the decision on a full exposition of the law on the question falling for its consideration, it becomes the law declared by the High Court and a precedent for future cases to be decided by the Tribunals." The Full Bench held that "the afore-mentioned conclusions of the Supreme Court found in its decision in Jyotsna Dey's case (supra) cannot be regarded as 'law declared' under Article 141 of the Constitution." Consequently, the Court decided that "the decision of this Court in H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma ... does not stand overruled by the decisions of the Supreme Court in Rajendra Kumari v. Shanta Trivedi and in Jyotsna Dey and Ors. v. State of Assam and Ors." The Court qualified this by emphasising that Supreme Court observations are binding only insofar as they constitute the ratio and a full exposition of law, and that factual conclusions not framed as general principles cannot be treated as 'law declared'. The Full Bench answered the reference by holding that H.T. Bhandary v. Muniyamma does not stand overruled by the Supreme Court decisions in Rajendra Kumari and Jyotsna Dey, and the contrary view taken by the Division Bench in General Manager, KSRTC v. Khatujabee is overruled. This is a Karnataka High Court Full Bench reference questioning whether the Court's earlier Bhandary decision on the 'operative multiplier' for loss of dependency in fatal motor-accident cases was impliedly overruled by two Supreme Court decisions (Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari). The Full Bench held that Bhandary's operative multiplier principle (basic multiplier 20, operative 16 for average cases) remains the law declared by the High Court and that the Supreme Court decisions cited were fact-specific and did not constitute 'law declared' under Article 141. The Full Bench overruled the contrary Division Bench decision in Khatujabee and confirmed that Tribunals may continue to apply the operative multiplier guidance. O.V. Shanthakumari v. Holding: Bhandary remains law declared by this Court; Jyotsna Dey and Rajendra Kumari were fact-specific and not 'law declared' under Article 141; Khatujabee's contrary view is overruled. Key legal points: Article 141 scope, Section 110E MV Act reference to just compensation, multiplier selection adjusted cumulatively for contingencies; no fresh compensation awarded by this judgment.
